metadata.title: Exploring climate change discourses on the internet: a topic modeling study across ten years

abstract: This study examines online discourses about climate change using automated text analysis. Samples of web contributions from various sources, such as blogs, online newspaper articles, readers ' comments, tweets, and others, were collected in 2013, 2019, and 2023. The major themes people talk about when discussing climate change on the internet were identified using structural topic modeling. The study also explored how people discuss climate change differently when using different types of media channels and how the main themes change over time. Six major topics were identified for each point in time; associations between topics and media types were examined. Three topics emerged repeatedly: Dispute over the reality and the implications of climate change, discussions about aspects of the science of climate change, and discussions about causes and consequences of global warming such as sea level rise. In 2019 and 2023, the topic of action (what to do) and the topic of looming disaster showed up repeatedly. Topics limited to one specific corpus were policy discussions, debates about emissions, about renewable energies, about agricultural implications, and expressions of despair. The topical structure could be organized in two general groups, one group containing more science-related topics, and another group containing expressions of dispute, disagreement, and affect. A significant correspondence between media type and prevalent topic could be detected. Media such as Twitter/X and comments are dominated by dispute and despair expressions, whereas media such as blogs and journalistic online articles are primarily concerned with scientific topics in general, as well as with specific topics concerning global warming or renewable energies. In sum, we found noteworthy topical invariances across ten years, and systematic correspondences between topics and media types. The study contributes to the understanding of online discourses about climate change and highlights the role of media type in shaping these discourses.

sections.paper_id: Böhm and Pfister - 2025 - Exploring climate change discourses on the internet a topic modeling study across ten years
paragraphs: Although the reality of climate change as well as the fact that it is mainly caused by human activities is broadly accepted among scientists (IPCC 2023), it has been and still is the subject of public controversy, and sometimes triggers fierce disputes involving the lay public, politicians, scientists, lobbyists and representatives of a variety of ideological backgrounds (Dunlap, McCright, and Yarosh 2016;Guber 2013;Hoffman 2011;McCright, Dunlap, and Marquart-Pyatt 2016;Poortinga et al. 2011Poortinga et al. , 2019;;Schäfer 2012;Ziegler 2017). How individuals perceive climate change and how beliefs about its causes and consequences are formed, and if and how people act upon climate change and other environmental risks is a salient research topic (Böhm 2003;Böhm and Pfister 2001, 2005, 2008, 2017;Bostrom et al. 2012;Doran et al. 2019;Jones and Song 2014;Poortinga et al. 2019;Steg 2023), suggesting that culture, basic beliefs, values, and emotions play a crucial role (Böhm et al. 2023;Jones 2011). Much of the public debate that affects and forms individual beliefs and values takes place on the internet, including more traditional media such as online newspapers and online magazines as well as social media such as blogs, Twitter (now X) and Facebook. Public opinion and public behavior concerning climate change, for example, social and individual attempts towards more sustainable consumption or less usage of fossil fuels, are strongly influenced and transformed by the way the public discourse unfolds on the internet (Pearce et al. 2019;Schäfer 2012;Williams et al. 2015).

The internet in general and social media in particular have transformed the way information about political, economic, and other social issues is distributed and understood by the public (Carr and Hayes 2015), and how risks such as climate change are communicated and perceived (Kasperson et al. 2022). Unlike traditional media (printed newspapers, television, radio, etc.) the internet allows people to (i) access information from a much broader and more diverse set of sources, and (ii) to generate and contribute own content in a very direct manner. A positive consequence might be that the public is better informed about an issue such as climate change, and that the possibility to provide content and to communicate online increases opportunities of democratic participation. However, this also opens the doors for fake news and conspiracy theories, enables the formation of self-reinforcing echo chambers, and works as an amplifier of populist propaganda and hate speech; if the negative effects on politics will outweigh the positive effects is still an open question (Nyhan et al. 2023;Zhuravskaya, Petrova, and Enikolopov 2020).

In public discourses about climate change we find deliberative discussions about scientific issues or about adequate strategies to minimize detrimental consequences as well as affective and hateful allegations, claiming that climate change is a hoax or a conspiracy. A central theoretical question is whether there is a specific relationship between the techno-economic architecture of a media format and the type of social interaction that unfolds (Walsh 2020). Some web-based media such as online newspapers still resemble traditional newspapers, that is, trained journalists contribute articles or play the role of gatekeepers to filter out fake-news. On the other hand, social media such as Twitter/X and Facebook are mainly based on user-generated content, with very low entry barriers, and a lack of qualified fact-checking mechanisms. In addition, as social media implement a resharing economy that rewards short, emotional, and meretricious messages (Lünenborg 2019;Robertson et al. 2023;Van Dijck and Poell 2013;Walsh 2020), the very architecture of the medium might cause discourses to be less deliberative and fact-based and be more affective and preference-based. It can be argued that social media, being owned by private companies seeking to maximize profit, foster simplified high-affective communications to increase click-rates, thus supporting the distribution of populistic viewpoints and fake-news (Tarnoff 2022;Vosoughi, Roy, and Aral 2018).

On a general level, the variety of different internet media channels can be distinguished along four dimensions (Zhuravskaya, Petrova, and Enikolopov 2020): (i) Amount of user-generated versus expert-generated content, (ii) low versus high entry barriers, and (iii) spread of information: uncontrolled resharing versus fact-checking; obviously, these dimensions are correlated. As a consequence, the generated content will more or less differ along a dimension of (iv) affective/preference-based to deliberative/fact-based (Zhuravskaya, Petrova, and Enikolopov 2020). This discrepancy is reminiscent of Kahneman's (2011) distinction between two modes of thinking: System-1 thinking which is fast, affective, and intuitive, and System-2 thinking, which is slow, deliberative, and rational. The affordances of internet media can be approximately aligned with this System-1/System-2 distinction, as media such as Twitter/X or Instagram encourage fast and affective responses, whereas blogs or online news articles require deliberation and cognitive effort.

Although these findings allow for plausible conjectures how climate change might be discussed in particular internet media channels, an explicit theory is yet to be developed; hence, we refrain from formulating specific hypotheses and proceed in a strictly explorative manner. We aim to contribute to the understanding of online discourses about climate change by using automated text analysis to analyze samples of web contributions from various sources such as blogs, online newspaper articles, and readers' comments on articles. We address the following issues: (i) What are the major themes people talk about when discussing climate change on the internet; (ii) do people discuss climate change differently when using different types of media channels; and (iii) how do the main themes change over time? Samples of text contributions from the internet were collected in 2013, in 2019, and in 2023. Each text corpus was analyzed via topic modeling, and six major topics were identified for each point in time. We examined how topics and type of media (that is, blogs, tweets, comments, etc.) are related, and we detected typical associations between topics and media types. A comparison of the three corpora shows how climate discourses changed over a time span of ten years.

Empirical research examining the structure and content of the climate change debate on the internet via topic modeling is not huge but growing during the past years. Chen, Molder, et al. (2023) collected five million tweets from climate activists and 30,000 news articles about climate change and climate strike from 2018 to 2021. Using LDA (Latent Dirichlet Allocation; see Section 2.3) topic modeling (Blei, Ng, and Jordan 2003) they found eight major themes representing the climate strike discourse on Twitter, 40 topics for US news media, and fourteen topics for global news, respectively. Specifically, they showed that the three actors (activists, US news, global news) have a few common themes, but also differ on major topics: US news focus on partisan debates, elections, and alarming consequences of climate change, global news focus on consequences of climate change as well, but also on climate protests and climate justice issues; climate activists emphasize topics such as solutions to climate change, climate consequences, collective actions, justice and education. Ylä-Anttila, Eranti, and Kukkonen (2022) analyzed newspaper pieces about climate change from one US and one Indian newspaper between 1997 and 2011, utilizing topics as derived from topic modeling as proxies for frames (Nisbet 2009). A topic modeling analysis using the MALLET tool kit (McCallum 2002) yielded twelve topics, interpreted as frames, and the authors showed that different actors such as NGOs, experts, and governments use different frames when talking about climate change. Al-Rawi, Kane, and Bizimana (2021) analyzed fifteen million tweets from 2015 to 2018 referencing 'climate change' and 'global warming' via topic modeling. They obtained a highly polarized topic structure; specifically, they found that those individuals who use the term 'climate change' tend to believe that climate change is anthropogenic, while individuals who use the term 'global warming' tend to believe in natural causes. Gaytan Camarillo et al. ( 2021) also studied tweets collected at three time points during the Covid-19 pandemic in 2020. Using LDA topic modeling, for each point in time nine to twelve topics were identified; some of them did not change much over time such as the 'energy' topic, others did change, such as 'action' and 'environmental behavior' which increased in frequency during the study period. Though not using contributions directly from internet sources, Tvinnereim and Fløttum (2015) applied structural topic modeling to study public understanding of climate change by analyzing open ended responses from a questionnaire study ('what comes to mind when you hear the words 'climate change'?'). They found four topics marked as weather/ice, future/impact, money/consumption, and attribution/causes (see also Tvinnereim et al. 2023). Notably, the authors correlated gender, age, and education with topic prevalence, showing that females are less likely to use the attribution topic and display less skepticism, that younger individuals are more likely to use the future/impact topic, and that education plays a lesser role in topic selection than expected. This study was one of the first using structural topic modeling to analyze how topic prevalence is related to explanatory variables such as gender and age. Further studies using topic modeling in the context of climate change, using internet contributions or other textual sources, are Benites-Lazaro, Giatti, andGiarolla (2018), DiMaggio, Nag, andBlei (2013), Grundmann (2021), Kukkonen, Stoddart, andYlä-Anttila (2021), andSalway (2017).

These studies and others are very diverse concerning the textual material collected, and concerning the specific procedure used for topic modeling. No standard approach has emerged so far, not to mention a coherent theory of climate change discourse in the digital world. To the best of our knowledge, no study up to now has used the full heterogeneity of internet sources and aimed at establishing a correspondence between internet source (what we call media type) and topical discourse structure.

sections.paper_id: Böhm and Pfister - 2025 - Exploring climate change discourses on the internet a topic modeling study across ten years
paragraphs: The sampling of documents from the internet followed the same approach for all three time points. Sampling was not automated but carried out manually. Three student research assistants collected documents from a variety of internet sources based on a standardized instruction (Appendix). We refrained from a purely automatic search for two reasons: The collection of texts should be clearly focused on the climate change debate as a common denominator, and we wanted a balanced mixture of documents from heterogenous sources, and not focus on just one source such as Twitter, or on one online category such as social media. Sampling a representative sample in the classical sense seems infeasible here since neither the population nor the drawing mechanism can be unambiguously defined. Hence, the research assistants were instructed to search for documents via Google using the keywords 'climate change' , 'global warming' , 'climate skeptics' , but also to visit websites they knew or would expect to be relevant, such as sites from NGOs or political parties. From any source document only those parts were to be extracted that explicitly dealt with climate change, and irrelevant parts were to be discarded. Most important, the instruction stated 'We want a maximum of diversity of contributions from many different web sources' , as one of our main research goals was to compare topics from different sources. We call this procedure 'pseudo-random purposeful sampling' , as there is a random element from Google search and from the idiosyncrasies of the research assistants, and there is a clear-cut purpose that directs the search from which sources to sample documents (Flick 2022;Palinkas et al. 2015).

In the second step, the sampled documents were decomposed by the research assistants into separate segments; these segments will be called 'text units' in the following. We define a text unit as a piece of text which expresses a more or less coherent thought or a coherently connected line of argument. Typically, a text unit corresponds to a paragraph, but can also be a single sentence or a few closely connected paragraphs (for full instruction see Appendix). This segmentation was done in order to increase the homogeneity of texts entering the analysis as basic units, and to get rid of irrelevant parts such as links, commercials, and other uninformative clutter. This collection of text units constitutes the elements of the text corpus for topic modeling.

paragraphs: The 2013 text corpus consisted of 1055 text units, derived from 261 documents. The mean number of words was 83.2 (SD = 44.1) overall, with a range from a minimum of five words to a maximum of 294 words. The sampling procedure focused on three media types: Blogs, comments (from comments sections of online newspapers, discussion groups, etc.), and journalism (journalistic articles from online news magazines). Table 1 shows the distribution of media types and mean word counts.

sections.paper_id: Böhm and Pfister - 2025 - Exploring climate change discourses on the internet a topic modeling study across ten years
paragraphs: Topic modeling is an inductive unsupervised statistical methodology to infer topics from a large corpus of text documents. It has become popular since the seminal work of Blei, Ng, and Jordan (2003), Blei (2012), and Blei and Lafferty (2007), who introduced the LDA algorithm (Latent Dirichlet Allocation); since then, many variants and further developments have been proposed (Chen, Peng, et al. 2023;Grimmer, Roberts, and Stewart 2021;Grimmer and Stewart 2013;Roberts, Stewart, and Airoldi 2016;Roberts, Stewart, and Tingley 2019;Vayansky and Kumar 2020). Conceptually, a topic represents a general theme which drives the communication and generates words and phrases that are semantically related. Informally, a topic is characterized by a set of words repeatedly occurring together; formally, a topic is represented by a probability distribution across a vocabulary of words, and those words that have the highest probability express the essential meaning of the topic. A text can be a mixture of several topics, and topics commonly have different prevalences (that is, different proportions of words from that topic will turn up in a text). A particular text with a very high proportion of a specific topic can be considered typical for that topic. The aim of topic modeling is to find those sets of words that frequently co-occur and thus indicate a topic, and to infer the underlying topics by identifying the common themes organizing the discourse as represented in the text corpus; researchers express a topic usually by assigning a short label that describes the topic, similar to identifying latent factors in factor analysis.

Introductions can be found in Blei (2012), Chen et al. (2023), andMaier et al. (2018). We use a special variant of topic modeling, Structural Topic Modeling (STM), introduced by Roberts et al. (2013) and available in the R package stm (Roberts, Stewart, and Tingley 2019). STM provides the possibility to include covariates, that is, quantitative or qualitative variables describing the analyzed documents. Thus, the topic prevalence for a particular document may vary depending on the value of the covariate; here, our covariate is media type (that is, the type of internet source) such as blog or tweet, and we allow prevalence estimation to vary with the media type. For estimation, we applied the SAGE (Sparse Additive GEnerative) model variant (Eisenstein, Ahmed, and Xing 2011) as implemented in the stm-package, an algorithmic modification which estimates word probabilities as deviations from an overall baseline frequency, often yielding more stable and interpretable topics (May et al. 2015;Wesslen 2018). However, these technical details do not alter the central objective of a topic modeling analysis, namely, to detect collections of words that typically and frequently co-occur in the text documents under study, and to assign sensible interpretations in terms of 'topic labels' summarizing the common meaning of these words.

For all analyses, we use the statistical environment R (R Core Team 2023). Our analytic approach proceeds, by and large, along the lines proposed by Maier et al. (2018), and also draws on Welbers, Van Atteveldt, and Benoit (2017; see also Weston et al. 2023). We proceed in three main steps: (i) Pre-Processing, that is, first cleaning the raw text documents and then standardizing the textual data in a way suitable for computational analysis; (ii) Analysis: Selecting an algorithm and parameters to estimate the model; (iii) Interpretation of topics and validation of results. The general procedure is described below, details are reported in the specific results sections.

paragraphs: All text units were thoroughly cleaned; specifically, html tags, hashtags, escape characters, punctuation, and the like were removed, surrounding white space was stripped, and digits, emojis and other symbols deleted. Common English stopwords (e. g., 'the' , 'and' , etc.) were removed. Texts were then tokenized to words, and normalized via lower case transformation and stemming to a vocabulary of terms consisting of lower case word stems. A document-term matrix was constructed, words with less than three characters and low frequency words were filtered out (that is, words occurring in fewer than four documents, and occurring less then three times in total). Pre-processing reduced the overall number of words substantially compared to the original word counts. The reduced document-term matrix served as input to topic analysis and was converted to stm-format. Pre-processing was done with the R packages textclean (Rinker 2018) and quanteda (Benoit et al. 2018).

A document-term matrix was constructed, words with less than three characters and low frequency words were filtered out (that is, words occurring in fewer than four documents, and occurring less then three times in total). Pre-processing reduced the overall number of words substantially compared to the original word counts. The reduced document-term matrix served as input to topic analysis and was converted to stm-format. Pre-processing was done with the R packages textclean (Rinker 2018) and quanteda (Benoit et al. 2018).

paragraphs: Topic analysis was performed using the R package stm (Roberts, Stewart, and Tingley 2019). Based on a series of test runs using three to nine topics, the number of topics was set to six, media type was used as covariate, and the algorithm used was SAGE (stm LDAbeta parameter was set to FALSE, thus estimating word probabilities as deviations from the corpus baseline frequencies; Eisenstein, Ahmed, and Xing 2011). Setting a constant seed number and using the spectral algorithm as recommended by Roberts, Stewart, and Tingley (2019) yields deterministic and replicable results.

sections.paper_id: Böhm and Pfister - 2025 - Exploring climate change discourses on the internet a topic modeling study across ten years
paragraphs: For interpretation of topics, we focus on those words which are most probable for the respective topic (so called PROB words), and additionally on those words which are particularly unique and exclusive for a topic (so called FREX words). For each topic the most probable words are simply computed as the conditional probability PROB = P(word|topic). Exclusivity is computed as a word's probability given a topic k divided by the sum of the word's probabilities across all topics (PROB/sum(PROB)); the FREX metric is a weighted sum of probability and exclusivity. More precisely, we look at the twelve words for which the probabilities are highest for a topic k, and we look at the twelve words for which the weighted mean of probability and exclusivity is highest (FREX words). We also inspect the most prototypical text units to support interpretation.

Ultimately, interpretation of topic meaning and the assignment of a summarizing label to characterize a topic is a subjective activity (Chen, Peng, et al. 2023); transparency of the process is thus key here. It is usually not possible to find a coherent interpretation that applies to each and every top word and all prototypical text units; it is unavoidable that some high frequency words are uninformative, or that some text units do not fit into the overall semantic space.

sections.paper_id: Böhm and Pfister - 2025 - Exploring climate change discourses on the internet a topic modeling study across ten years
paragraphs: Topic model estimation requires the number of topics as a fixed input parameter. The choice of how many topics to estimate is notoriously difficult (Weston et al. 2023), and as has been noted before, there is not a 'correct' number (Chen, Peng, et al. 2023;Maier et al. 2018). Two criteria determined our choice: First, the number of topics should be fairly small as we aimed at more general themes that might be relevant across a time span of ten years, rather than microscopic issues of only temporary interest. Therefore, we examined a range from three to nine topics. Second, diagnostic measures of a model's quality should be acceptable, in particular, semantic coherence and exclusivity should be in the upper range for the selected number of k topics for all three corpora.

Using the searchK-function from the stm package, for each corpus a series of models was run, with topic numbers ranging from three to nine. Exclusivity and semantic coherence are shown in Table 4. Exclusivity basically increases with topic number, whereas semantic coherence shows no clear regularity, but is getting low for topic numbers greater than six. For subsequent analyses we selected the topic number k = 6 as an adequate compromise between the two criteria.

paragraphs: The collection of text units as described in Section 2.2.1 underwent all pre-processing procedures as outlined in Section 2.3.1. After pre-processing, the reduced collection consisted of 1054 text units (one text unit had to be discarded because of zero elements) and a vocabulary of 1892 terms (stemmed words). The mean number of terms per text was 35.4 (SD = 18.8) with a median of 33 terms. For media types blog, comment, and journalism, the mean and median were 34.7 and 32 for blogs, 34.9 and 32 for comments, and 36.1 and 34 for journalistic articles, respectively. The document-feature-matrix was converted to stm-format and submitted to a structural topic model analysis as specified in Section 2.3.2.

sections.paper_id: Böhm and Pfister - 2025 - Exploring climate change discourses on the internet a topic modeling study across ten years
paragraphs: For interpretation, we refer to the top words of each topic, as well as to typical text units; specifically, we inspect the most probable terms for each topic (PROB words), and the most exclusive terms (FREX words) (see Section 2.3.3). Table 5 shows the top words for each topic as well as topic prevalence and exclusivity and semantic coherence measures. The first line shows the label we assigned to the topic to summarize the topic's core gist.

The first topic includes words associated with typical phenomena of climate change, especially with its consequences (weather, increase, sea, level, rise, drought, storm, precipitation). A snippet from a typical text (Appendix) is 'Increasingly variable rainfall patterns are likely to affect the supply of fresh water. A lack of safe water can compromise hygiene and increase the risk of diarrhoeal disease …'; typical texts show a very factual and scientific style. We assign the label SCIENCE to this topic, as it is characterized by scientific considerations of climate change and its consequences.

The second topic can be labeled as representing DISPUTE, that is, disputes about the reality of climate change, including denials, or about the severity of its consequences, and the like. Top words suggest discussions of believability and denial, and the presence of linguistic hedges (just, now, like, real, still) suggests that this is a topic characterized by subjective opinion and fuzziness. A snipped from a typical text unit 'But what the green happy clappers do not get is they have been sold one giant pup. They love it so much, it is so much a part of what they are, it is their big-eyed life-style brand-of-choice that they fail to open their minds to the fact that they have been duped …' implies that climate change is considered a hoax by the author.

The third topic focuses mostly on the international level and on governmental policies concerning climate change (nation, country, government, economy, plan). A typical text contains the fragment ' … A legally binding agreement would ensure that richer nations provide adequate financial and technical support to enable the poorest countries to adapt to climate change and The fourth topic is clearly focused on the phenomenon of the greenhouse effect (atmosphere, CO 2 , earth, ice, gas, heat, melt), hence a suitable label would be GREENHOUSE. Typical texts include explicit explanations such as ' … The term greenhouse is used in conjunction with the phenomenon known as the greenhouse effect. Energy from the sun drives the earth's weather and climate, and heats the earth's surface …' .

The fifth topic is closely related to the greenhouse topic, focusing on the most salient consequence of global warming (warm, global, temperature, record, trend, degree); typical texts again often include explanations: '… As environmental engineer, D Kelly O Day, writes on ProcessingTrends.com explains: To facilitate assessments of long term trends, climatologists compare the mean for a base period with the annual mean …' . We label this topic simply GLOBAL WARMING.

The sixth topic is primarily about emissions caused by fossil fuels or other pollutions from industrial production; also, issues of emission reduction or prices on fossil fuels are involved (carbon, gas, emission, pollution, fuel, industry, power, fossil). A typical text unit mentions possible causes of CO 2 emissions: ' … In newly constructed buildings, production of materials used in building and energy used during construction are sited as the cause of carbon dioxide emissions. …' . For simplicity, we label this topic just EMISSION.

As can be seen in Table 5, these topics do not differ much on semantic coherence and exclusivity; also, the prevalences are fairly similar, with topic DISPUTE showing the largest prevalence (0.23) and GREENHOUSE the lowest prevalence (0.12). The stm-algorithm allows topics to correlate, which can of course be expected in natural communication. In order to explore the interrelations of the topics, estimates of topic correlations were converted to distances and submitted to a hierarchical clustering algorithm (Figure 1). Apparently, there are two high-level topics, SCIENCE and DISPUTE, suggesting that discussions on the internet about climate change roughly revolve around either scientific issues, or around contentious issues such as the reality of climate change, including appropriate strategies on how to deal with climate change.

sections.header_raw: Association between media type and topics
sections.header_norm: association between media type and topics
paragraphs: The collection from 2013 distinguishes three media types: Blogs, comments, and journalistic articles. For analysis, for each combination of topic and media type the mean prevalence was computed. The resulting cross-tabulation was treated as a quasi-contingency table, that is, larger numbers indicate a more frequent association between a specific topic and a specific media type. Specifically, we computed the quasi-frequency of a topic k and a media type i occurring together as f x i k i k , , = ⋅100 (with x i k , being the mean probability of topic k occurring in text units of type i, across all text units).

For interpretation, the contingency table (Table 6) was submitted to a correspondence analysis (Greenacre 2016;Nenadic and Greenacre 2007); the resultant two-dimensional biplot is depicted in Figure 2 and shows the associations between topics and media types; media types are represented as blue arrows, and topics as red triangles. The origin represents an hypothetical average topic, that is, a topic with average associations to all media types; the further away a topic is from the origin, the more specific it is with respect to a media type. The projection of a topic point onto the media type vector indicates the relative degree of association, that is, the relative prevalence of that topic in text units of the respective media type. Evidently, the DISPUTE topic is most prevalent in comments, the SCIENCE and GREENHOUSE topics show up mostly in blogs, and in journalistic articles the topics EMISSON, Science, and POLICY are most prevalent; the GLOBAL WARMING topic, situated near the center of the configuration, is fairly evenly represented in all media types, though to a relatively lesser extent than the other topics.
In sum, these findings suggest noticeable differences between media types concerning their respective topic prevalence, with the association between DISPUTE and comments being especially salient.

The projection of a topic point onto the media type vector indicates the relative degree of association, that is, the relative prevalence of that topic in text units of the respective media type. Evidently, the DISPUTE topic is most prevalent in comments, the SCIENCE and GREENHOUSE topics show up mostly in blogs, and in journalistic articles the topics EMISSON, Science, and POLICY are most prevalent; the GLOBAL WARMING topic, situated near the center of the configuration, is fairly evenly represented in all media types, though to a relatively lesser extent than the other topics.

In sum, these findings suggest noticeable differences between media types concerning their respective topic prevalence, with the association between DISPUTE and comments being especially salient.

paragraphs: As with the 2013 corpus, the text units in the 2019 corpus underwent all pre-processing procedures as outlined in Section 2.3.1. After pre-processing, the reduced collection consisted of 1986 text units and a vocabulary of 3691 terms. Mean number of terms per text was 59.8 (SD = 50.9) with a median of 45 terms. The median number of terms for blogs was 66, for comments 25, for journalistic articles 76, for opinion pieces 60, for organization texts 67, for popular science articles 77, for tweets 16, and for the residual category 61. The document-feature-matrix was converted to stm-format and submitted to a structural topic model analysis as specified in Section 2.3.2. As outlined above, we extracted six topics, and interpreted and labeled the topics based on top words (PROB and FREX), and on extracts from typical text units (Table 7; for text extracts see Appendix).

sections.paper_id: Böhm and Pfister - 2025 - Exploring climate change discourses on the internet a topic modeling study across ten years
paragraphs: The first topic is to a large extent affected by the advent of Greta Thunberg and the Fridays for Future movement. Top words include 'protest' , 'action' , 'strike' , 'children' , 'rebellion' , and the name 'Thunberg'; these words suggest that the topic is focused on issues of protest and activities concerning climate change, for example, on organizing strike activities. We label this topic ACTION, that is, calls to action, strike, and other activities to either increase public and political attention, or to directly influence people's behavior to tackle climate change.

The second topic is about global warming and related phenomena such as sea level rise (top words: global, temperature, 'sea, rise, weather, etc.) and closely corresponds to the fifth topic from 2013; thus, we assign the same label GLOBAL WARMING. Typical text units include explanations and forecasts about the future development of global temperature and sea level rise.

The third topic revolves around all sorts of real and potential disasters associated with climate change, such as the collapse of glaciers, forest fires and related human fatalities. Top words include 'glacier' , 'fire' , 'mountain' , 'collapse' , and also the name Bolsonaro appears whose presidency started in 2019. We label this topic DISASTER.

The fourth topic is highly similar to the dispute topic from 2013 and we label it accordingly DISPUTE. As in 2013, it involves debates about the scientific status of climate change, consisting of deniers as well as activists, but seems somewhat more politicized (top words include the name 'Trump' , 'democrats' , 'republicans' , 'consensus' , as well as 'scientific' , 'global' , 'human'). We also find typical contributions ridiculing climate science ('Climate Science … the canonical name of the religious dogma of the Global Warming mythology …').

The fifth topic, with a high prevalence of 0.24, represents articulations from individuals who feel hopelessness and despair about climate change and resignation about what could be done. The top words are not very informative (can, people, just, thing, think, stop), though it is evident from inspecting typical text fragments that the basic theme refers to subjective expressions of resignation, but also to calls for action (' … Stop making-having babies …'). We summarize the topic with the label DESPAIR. A topic that was not prevalent in 2013 is Topic 6 which is about the issue of RENEWABLE energies (top words include 'emission' , 'energy' , 'carbon' , 'fuel' , 'renewable' , 'fossil' , 'technology'); also, the connection with economic measures such as taxation is mentioned.

In sum, we find two familiar topics from 2013, GLOBAL WARMING and DISPUTE, and four new topics, two of which concerning the increasing seriousness of climate change (DISASTER, DESPAIR), and two topics about how one should deal with the problem (appeals to action, renewable energies).

As with the 2013 study, we computed topic correlations and submitted the correlations to a hierarchical cluster analysis (Figure 3). The dendrogram shows that DISPUTE and DESPAIR are closely associated, and can be distinguished from the general topic of GLOBAL WARMING which is associated with RENEWABLE energies, as well as a cluster joining the topics ACTION and DISASTER. Apparently, a general dichotomy arises in climate change discussions opposing more pessimistic and skeptic voices (DISPUTE and DESPAIR) with more encouraging contributions containing appeals for action and discussions about the potential of renewable energies.

sections.header_raw: Association between media type and topics
sections.header_norm: association between media type and topics
paragraphs: The correspondence analysis of the cross-tabulation of media types and topics (Table 8; see Section 3.1.2) is shown in Figure 4. The topics DISPUTE and DESPAIR are clearly linked with tweets, and to a slightly lesser extent with comments; this is not too surprising as in 2019 Trump's tweets played a prominent role on Twitter, and were also frequently concerned with climate change issues, expressing denials of climate change. Twitter also emerges as the main 7 and 8 (corpus 2019). channel for despair expressions; as the topics DISPUTE and DESPAIR are correlated, Twitter appears to be the platform to communicate affective, strongly opinionated, non-factual messages. The ACTION and DISASTER topics are also correlated and clearly associated with journalistic articles and opinion pieces; appeals to action have been strongly driven by Greta Thunberg and the Fridays for Future movement, which has been picked up by the mainstream press and accordingly communicated in online journals. Discussions about the GLOBAL WARMING topic and related issues such as sea level rise is mainly covered by popular science outlets, and the discussion about renewable energies is relatively strong in journalistic articles, blogs, and other more specialized web sites. Compared to 2013, the 2019 corpus distinguished more media types, so the results are not directly comparable. However, again a relatively clear-cut correspondence between topics and media types emerged, and all topics can be assigned to one or two specific media types.

paragraphs: As before, the text units underwent all pre-processing procedures as outlined in Section 2.3.1. After pre-processing, the reduced collection consisted of 1281 text units and a vocabulary of 2985 terms. The mean number of terms per text was 76.7 (SD = 55.0) with a median of 67 terms. The median number of terms for blogs was 97, for comments 13, for journalistic articles 78, for discussion sites 45, and for tweets 19. The document-feature-matrix was converted to stm-format and submitted to a structural topic model analysis as specified in Section 2.3.2. As outlined above, six topics were extracted, and interpretation was based on top words (PROB and FREX, Table 9), and on typical text units (Table 9 Appendix).

sections.paper_id: Böhm and Pfister - 2025 - Exploring climate change discourses on the internet a topic modeling study across ten years
paragraphs: Top words are shown in Table 9 and examples of typical texts are included in the Appendix.

The first topic, characterized by terms such as 'weather' , 'health' , 'heat' , 'extreme' , 'drought' , 'hurricane' , and 'death' suggest that it is mainly about extreme weather events caused by climate change. The typical text units contain descriptions of weather events such as droughts and wildfires and their impact on humans. This parallels the disaster topic from the 2019 corpus and we label it accordingly DISASTER. The second topic (top words include 'ice' , 'sea' , 'glacier' , 'temperature' , 'warm' , 'melt') is about global warming and its consequences, in particular melting of glaciers and sea level rise; hence, we assign the label GLOBAL WARMING. The third topic is broadly concerned with scientific achievements and findings, about the dynamics of climate change but also about its effects and about measures and technologies to mitigate climate change and to adapt to its irreversible effects (top words include 'emissions' , 'warning' , 'risk' , 'reports' , 'IPCC' , 'economics'); a typical text for this topic is about how to manage disasters: ' … further investments are needed for resilience, and of the enhancements required to improve disaster preparedness …' . We label this thematically comprehensive topic SCIENCE, because typically the starting point in these texts is some scientific result. The fourth topic, characterized by top words such as 'action' , 'need' , 'power' , 'renewable' , 'transition' , and 'government' , contains appeals to action as well as reports about ongoing activities tackling climate change. Social and political as well as technological aspects are discussed; we label this topic simply ACTION. The fifth topic is about a special but important issue in the climate change debate, which is agriculture. Top words include 'food' , 'crop' , 'agriculture' , 'carbon' 'farmer' , and 'product'; a typical text, for example, deals with means of sustainable production. Accordingly, we assign the label AGRICULTURE. The sixth topic can again be labeled DISPUTE, as it contains contributions from skeptics, deniers, and their antagonists; top words in the DISPUTE topic are usually short and insubstantial, such as 'people' , 'think' , 'like' , 'believe' , 'know' , or 'really' , but inspecting typical text sections reveals confrontational communications between climate change believers and skeptics. A hierarchical cluster analysis of topic correlations shows on a general level a plausible opposition of two clusters, one containing the topics SCIENCE, GLOBAL WARMING, DISASTER, and AGRICULTURE, the other containing the ACTION and DISPUTE topics (Figure 5). On the one hand, we find a high level cluster including topics that revolve around factual and genuine issues associated with climate change (e.g., how to handle weather disasters, how to assist sustainable farming), on the other hand, we find topics concerning disagreements about what to do or about more essential differences about the status of climate change.

On the one hand, we find a high level cluster including topics that revolve around factual and genuine issues associated with climate change (e.g., how to handle weather disasters, how to assist sustainable farming), on the other hand, we find topics concerning disagreements about what to do or about more essential differences about the status of climate change.

sections.paper_id: Böhm and Pfister - 2025 - Exploring climate change discourses on the internet a topic modeling study across ten years
paragraphs: The data collection contains three points in time spanning ten years, thus allowing a long-term comparison of climate change topics. We compare topics on an abstract level, referring to the semantic labels we assigned previously. Because data collection was not strictly parallel across time (see Section 2.1), and because there is no unique one-to-one mapping of semantic interpretation and word co-occurrences (that is, even quite different sets of words may refer to the same topic), a comparison on the level of concrete words seems inappropriate. Table 11 presents an overview of the six topics for each year; the two most prevalent topics of each year are in 9 and Table 10). bold font, and topics that emerge twice or three times are grayed. Five topics emerge repeatedly, the topic SCIENCE in 2013 and 2023, the topics ACTION and DISASTER in 2019 and 2023, and the topics GLOBAL WARMING and DISPUTE at all three points in time. There is a remarkable constancy across ten years concerning what deems to be a relevant topic of discussion: The core consequence of climate change which is global warming, and an ongoing dispute about the causes and political implications of climate change, mainly driven by so called deniers and their opponents, often activists. More specific, though still very broad, topics such as the greenhouse effect, emissions, or agricultural issues are less enduring. One noteworthy trend that is suggested by the pattern of changing topics is from theory to action. Whereas in 2013 the greenhouse effect and the role of emissions appear as prominent topics, in 2019 and 2023 an obvious change in focus is towards action. That is, the question 'how does climate change work' is partly replaced by the pressing question 'what can be done' or even by appeals to action urging to 'do something' . The emergence of a disaster topic and a despair topic indicates another trend reflecting a wide-spread feeling among many groups of people that it is perhaps too late to substantially tackle climate change and that disastrous events will inevitably occur in the future.

All topics can be grouped in two broad and rather distinct categories, following the hierarchical clustering results. One group consists of discourses about scientific issues of climate change, about its causes and consequences, and includes more concrete issues such as the greenhouse effect or the transition to renewable energies. The second group consists of disputes about the reality and significance of climate change, about activities and policies to tackle or mitigate climate change, and about subjective sentiments of approaching disaster and despair (Table 11). In a very simplifying way this can be considered as factual versus affective discourses, and this dichotomy shows up at all three points of time.

The correspondence analyses of the associations between media type and topic (Figures 2, 4, 6) also show a notable invariance: Affective topics such as DISPUTE and DESPAIR are expressed on Twitter and on comment sites, that is, on media formats which provide easy access for contributions from the wider public, and which are built explicitly to express short and snappy messages. Scientific discussions and debates about strategies and policies are represented on quite different media types, such as online magazines and blogs. Usually, contributions for online magazines are written by journalists with at least a basic level of knowledge about the content and about the formal conventions of serious writing, or by bloggers who often invest huge amounts of time and effort to delve into the subject matter. Although the three text corpora from 2013, 2019, and 2023 are only partly comparable as they include different media type categories, the results suggest a clear and plausible correspondence between media type and topic. These correspondences persist over time and appear to become more polarized considering the pattern found in 2023 (Figure 6): Twitter, discussion sites and comment sites converge on presenting mainly opinionated disputes about climate change, whereas all the substantial and factual topics are discussed in blogs and journalistic articles.

sections.paper_id: Böhm and Pfister - 2025 - Exploring climate change discourses on the internet a topic modeling study across ten years
paragraphs: The issue of climate change and its causes and consequences has been widely accepted among scientists, but it continues to be a subject of public controversy and debate. Climate change constitutes a societal risk in two ways: It poses a risk to human society by objectively changing the global climate in harmful ways, and it represents a risk for societies as a cause of dispute and controversy between social camps holding opposing views about the reality, causes, and policy implications of climate change. Though evidence is mixed, climate change debates may be a contributing factor to an increasing polarization in public opinion and political ideology, and the digital sphere may amplify and accelerate this effect (Allgaier 2019;Elgesem, Steskal, and Diakopoulos 2017;Fellenor et al. 2018). This paper aims to contribute to the understanding of online discourses about climate change by using automated text analysis to analyze web contributions from various sources such as blogs, online newspaper articles, tweets, and readers' comments on articles. The paper addresses three main issues: identification of the major themes people talk about when discussing climate change on the internet, whether people discuss climate change differently when using different types of media channels, and how the main themes change over a period of ten years. Our approach is based on topic modeling and is explorative and agnostic (Grimmer, Roberts, and Stewart 2021). Topic modeling algorithms work inductively and are able to detect recurrent patterns of co-occurring words in large textual corpora. Thus, we contribute to the growing research literature applying topic modeling methodology to the issue of climate change, specifically, to discourses about climate change unfolding on the internet.

How political controversies develop is mostly determined by the way the public discourse unfolds in the media, in traditional print and broadcast media as well as increasingly on the internet. We define internet media as comprising social media (e.g. Twitter/X) as well as other web sites representing traditional news media or other organizations, and related online channels such as blogs and comments.

The crucial role of the media in amplifying or attenuating risks has been elaborated in the social amplification of risk framework (SARF) as introduced by Kasperson et al. (1988Kasperson et al. ( , 2022;;Pidgeon, Kasperson, and Slovic 2003). Initially developed before the ubiquity of the internet, Fellenor et al. (2018), among others, applied SARF to social media, regarding social media as so called amplification stations whose scope extends beyond the limits of traditional media, reaching a more heterogenous public and targeting more individualized audiences. However, the specifics of the internet as a diverse collection of amplification/attenuation stations has not been fully elaborated within the social amplification of risk framework. SARF has also little to say about long term developments as we study in this paper.

In sum, we find that discourses about climate change on the internet can be summarized with six topics. Over the time span of ten years, some of these topics change, others remain invariant. One invariant topic refers to dispute, representing arguments pro and contra the reality and significance of climate change, often including strong and extreme opinions, insults, polemic confrontations, and clashes of political attitudes and worldviews. Obviously, climate change is a general topic that remains highly controversial, a controversy that is frequently embodied by public figures such as Donald Trump or Greta Thunberg. Two other topics showing constancy over time are discussions about the scientific basics of climate change, and discussions about its major manifestation, global warming. Science and global warming are topics that regularly contain quite serious and accurate discussions about what is scientifically known about causes, consequences, and means to mitigate climate change. In recent years, there seems to be a tendency to focus more on specific aspects such as agricultural issues or technologies for renewable energies. Ignoring for the moment those people who still deny human made climate change or consider it as inconsequential, these tendencies may indicate a more pragmatic view that in addition to efforts to stop climate change per se, measures must also concentrate on slowing down and mitigating its harmful consequences.

Two further topics that emerge since 2019 are ACTION and DISASTER. Appeals to action, to actually do something, individually and politically, arise prominently since the appearance of Greta Thunberg and the Fridays for Future movements. It seems that this trend towards action is driven partly by increasingly vivid scenarios of looming disasters, such as droughts, floods, sea level rise, and glacier melting. This increasing action-orientation might reflect broader societal and political changes. The COP28 United Nations Climate Change Conference in Dubai in 2023 has, in spite of justified criticisms about greenwashing, and the lack of concrete and binding steps, basically agreed to move away from fossil fuels. Also in 2023, Pope Francis in his Laudate Deum exhortation explicitly condemned climate change denial and called for action to fight the climate crisis. Especially young people are increasingly calling for more forceful climate action (Pickard 2022). From groups such as Fridays for Future, activist movements such as the Last Generation have emerged, who focus more strongly on direct action and civil disobedience causing a lot of publicity and outrage. In Germany, the Green party as a member of the government coalition, tries to implement comprehensive measures such as the large-scale implementation of heat pumps, though with limited success as yet. It seems that large parts of society and politics have realized that the window of opportunity to avert the most disastrous consequences of climate change is closing, as is emphasized, for example, by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change's (IPCC) 6th assessment report (IPCC 2023). Correspondingly, disaster has become a quite conspicuous topic in online discourses. The awareness of possibly unavoidable catastrophes might be one cause for expressions of despair and hopelessness to increase, and thus form another distinct topic.

The closing window of opportunity to tackle climate change and the increasing urgency to take rapid action may also spark opposition. For example, a time series of national surveys in Norway shows that the public increasingly opposes the policy suggestion to ban fossil cars by 2025 as this date approaches (Gregersen 2023). A possible explanation for this trend is that the shrinking time perspective diverts attention from the goals that a policy is trying to achieve towards the practical intricacies of implementing a policy (see construal level theory, Trope and Liberman 2010). Also on a societal level, many places in Europe have seen fierce opposition to climate policies that are seen as being implemented too rapidly, for example the Gilets Jaunes protests in France in 2018. In current debates about climate action, discourses emerge that justify inaction and the delay of action, for example by emphasizing the downsides of climate policies (Lamb et al. 2020). Such developments could be reflected in our results by the fact that the DISPUTE topic remained stable, or even increased in prevalence.

A close correspondence was found between media type, that is, type of internet channel, and topic. The topic DISPUTE and to a lesser extent the topic DESPAIR dominate Twitter, comments, and similar discussion sites. The topic SCIENCE as well as the topic GLOBAL WARMING are a dominant theme in journalistic articles, in popular science sites, and also in blogs. We suggest a dichotomy of topics between science oriented discourses and dispute-conflict oriented discourses. This dichotomy also largely holds with respect to media types. On the one hand, online articles and blogs are primarily outlets for scientific, argument-based debates, whereas tweets and comments are the primary channel for communicating dissent, conflict, polemics, and affective expressions. This correspondence is quite stable across the three points in time being studied.

As mentioned in the introduction, this finding is not unexpected. It has been often observed that social media are in large parts characterized by aggressive, impolite, and provocative forms of communication (Lünenborg 2019;Pearce et al. 2019;Walsh 2020;Zhuravskaya, Petrova, and Enikolopov 2020), and explanations range from communicative features such as social distance and anonymity, to technical-economic features which reward short, affective, and sensationalist messages. In our study, these features apply to media types Twitter/X and comments, and dispute and despair show up primarily on these media. Consequently, skepticism and climate change denial will be prominent in social media; however, the DISPUTE topic indicates that social media are not simply outlets of climate change skepticism, but at the same time function as platforms for climate change activism; hence, even though the discourse might be described as rough and lacking good manners, we find various ideological camps involved in the discourse and the role of Twitter and other social media might be more complex (Diehl et al. 2021). However, important social media such as Facebook, TikTok, or Instagram have not been included in our study, so we must be careful with generalizations.

As pointed out in the introduction, a promising theoretical account for the dichotomy between fact-based and affect-based discourses is Kahneman's (2011;Morewedge and Kahneman 2010) two-systems theory. Kahneman suggests two modalities or systems of cognition which process information differently and generate different types of judgments and evaluations. System 1 operates largely in a fast and automatic fashion, based on associations, is effortless, and is closely linked to affect and perception. System 2 operates mainly under conscious control, proceeds according to rules, logic, and rational arguments, but is slow and requires cognitive effort. System 1 generates intuitive judgments based on gut feeling, and System 2 generates judgments based on deliberation and analysis. The notion of cognitive effort may provide a key dimension to understand differences in topic prevalence and media type usage. Internet media such as Twitter/X and most likely other social media channels such as WhatsApp or Facebook require little or no effort to use. Typically, short messages are expected, and the social norm is that these messages require no justification; the predominant response is purely preferential (like or dislike) and effortless, which may explain the popularity of these types of social media. In contrast, internet channels such as blogs (contributions of the principal author, not the comments) and longer journalistic contributions require some literacy and often a lot of effort, deliberation, and rational argumentation. The adequate response is epistemic (true or false), requiring a minimum of cognitive effort to prove a claim incorrect or an argument incoherent. Hence, we speculate that the psychological distinction between System 1 and System 2 is to some extent reflected in the topical structure and in the media type correspondences of climate change discussions on the internet.

Some shortcomings of this study should be noted. The sampling procedure can be criticized for being somewhat ad hoc and not yielding a representative sample of texts. As already mentioned in Section 2.1, this is indeed problematic, though we think that the notion of representative sampling is basically unattainable in this context. A related shortcoming is the relatively small number of texts in our corpora; topic analyses often process thousands or even millions of documents scraped from the internet. However, we think that the claim that the sheer amount of data is sufficient to generate insightful findings is unwarranted (Grimmer, Roberts, and Stewart 2021).

The method of topic modeling has some genuine drawbacks, as mentioned above (see Section 2.3). First, it is purely inductive or bottom-up, that is, the results do not depend on any preconceived theoretical assumptions about the topic structure; an implication is that standard algorithms are unable to distinguish relevant patterns from noise (for attempts towards a more top-down procedure see, for example, Watanabe and Baturo 2024). Second, technically, there are numerous degrees of freedom to control the analysis, most of them lack strong theoretical justification until now. The number of topics to be extracted must be fixed a priori, without clear rules what the 'correct' number might be; in fact, we think that a correct number does not exist. A specific estimation algorithm must be selected from a plethora of currently available algorithms with each algorithm providing many optional parameters that control the estimation process (Wiedemann 2022); also, since the estimation process usually involves some random element, the results are not deterministic. Furthermore, how to evaluate the quality of a topic model is still debated, though measures such as coherence and exclusivity are conventionally used, and steps toward a more standardized approach have been proposed (Chen, Peng, et al. 2023;Maier et al. 2018). Ultimately, topic models provide a helpful approach to manage huge amounts of textual data, but the conceptual interpretation remains subjective and a matter of expertise with the substantive field.

Overall, our findings contribute to an understanding of online discourses about climate change and how they have evolved over time. The analysis highlights the importance of considering different media types and their influence on public opinion and behavior related to climate change. However, it remains challenging to derive some clear advice on policy-making and strategic agenda setting. Online media consumption is mostly driven by factors that attract attention and trigger affective responses (Robertson et al. 2023). This has always been the case also with traditional media, but online social media may intensify this property (Kasperson et al. 2022). More importantly, as digital media more and more replace traditional media, especially with younger generations, it is essential that political strategists and journalists adapt to this development and learn how to better reach their audiences.

A major concern in climate change debates is the widespread use of misinformation and the undermining of the credibility of scientists. This is particularly common on social media due to the lack of 'gatekeepers' and the unrestricted distribution of user generated content. Lewandowsky (2021) discusses several strategies how to communicate in what he calls 'adversarial environments' , for example, using emotional messages, highlighting scientific consensus, and using inoculation and debunking strategies. These measures are all the more important in online media, and should be adapted to social media in particular.

Another concern is that so called echo chambers and filter bubbles are easily formed on the internet, such as homogeneous groups on Facebook or Instagram. In echo chambers, like-minded people communicate only or mostly with each other, thus confirming and reinforcing their opinions and shielding themselves from opposing views and arguments (Williams et al. 2015). It has been assumed that echo chambers account for ideological polarization and radicalization tendencies. However, recent research by Nyhan and colleagues (Nyhan et al. 2023) suggests that this assumption may not be justified. Manipulating the amount of like-minded information available to Facebook users had no effect on political attitudes, ideological extremity or affective polarization. Also, the amount of political content shared on Facebook is relatively low. Whether online debates are the causes of political polarization, especially when shaped by social media constraints and affordances, or mainly reflect (and possibly amplify) preexisting attitudes and convictions, is an open question. Our findings underscore the need for further research in this area, as the topic of climate change will continue to be a subject of public controversy and debate.

We define internet media as comprising social media (e.g. Twitter/X) as well as other web sites representing traditional news media or other organizations, and related online channels such as blogs and comments.

The crucial role of the media in amplifying or attenuating risks has been elaborated in the social amplification of risk framework (SARF) as introduced by Kasperson et al. (1988Kasperson et al. ( , 2022;;Pidgeon, Kasperson, and Slovic 2003). Initially developed before the ubiquity of the internet, Fellenor et al. (2018), among others, applied SARF to social media, regarding social media as so called amplification stations whose scope extends beyond the limits of traditional media, reaching a more heterogenous public and targeting more individualized audiences. However, the specifics of the internet as a diverse collection of amplification/attenuation stations has not been fully elaborated within the social amplification of risk framework. SARF has also little to say about long term developments as we study in this paper.

In sum, we find that discourses about climate change on the internet can be summarized with six topics. Over the time span of ten years, some of these topics change, others remain invariant. One invariant topic refers to dispute, representing arguments pro and contra the reality and significance of climate change, often including strong and extreme opinions, insults, polemic confrontations, and clashes of political attitudes and worldviews. Obviously, climate change is a general topic that remains highly controversial, a controversy that is frequently embodied by public figures such as Donald Trump or Greta Thunberg. Two other topics showing constancy over time are discussions about the scientific basics of climate change, and discussions about its major manifestation, global warming. Science and global warming are topics that regularly contain quite serious and accurate discussions about what is scientifically known about causes, consequences, and means to mitigate climate change. In recent years, there seems to be a tendency to focus more on specific aspects such as agricultural issues or technologies for renewable energies. Ignoring for the moment those people who still deny human made climate change or consider it as inconsequential, these tendencies may indicate a more pragmatic view that in addition to efforts to stop climate change per se, measures must also concentrate on slowing down and mitigating its harmful consequences.

Two further topics that emerge since 2019 are ACTION and DISASTER. Appeals to action, to actually do something, individually and politically, arise prominently since the appearance of Greta Thunberg and the Fridays for Future movements. It seems that this trend towards action is driven partly by increasingly vivid scenarios of looming disasters, such as droughts, floods, sea level rise, and glacier melting. This increasing action-orientation might reflect broader societal and political changes. The COP28 United Nations Climate Change Conference in Dubai in 2023 has, in spite of justified criticisms about greenwashing, and the lack of concrete and binding steps, basically agreed to move away from fossil fuels. Also in 2023, Pope Francis in his Laudate Deum exhortation explicitly condemned climate change denial and called for action to fight the climate crisis. Especially young people are increasingly calling for more forceful climate action (Pickard 2022). From groups such as Fridays for Future, activist movements such as the Last Generation have emerged, who focus more strongly on direct action and civil disobedience causing a lot of publicity and outrage. In Germany, the Green party as a member of the government coalition, tries to implement comprehensive measures such as the large-scale implementation of heat pumps, though with limited success as yet. It seems that large parts of society and politics have realized that the window of opportunity to avert the most disastrous consequences of climate change is closing, as is emphasized, for example, by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change's (IPCC) 6th assessment report (IPCC 2023). Correspondingly, disaster has become a quite conspicuous topic in online discourses. The awareness of possibly unavoidable catastrophes might be one cause for expressions of despair and hopelessness to increase, and thus form another distinct topic.

The closing window of opportunity to tackle climate change and the increasing urgency to take rapid action may also spark opposition. For example, a time series of national surveys in Norway shows that the public increasingly opposes the policy suggestion to ban fossil cars by 2025 as this date approaches (Gregersen 2023). A possible explanation for this trend is that the shrinking time perspective diverts attention from the goals that a policy is trying to achieve towards the practical intricacies of implementing a policy (see construal level theory, Trope and Liberman 2010). Also on a societal level, many places in Europe have seen fierce opposition to climate policies that are seen as being implemented too rapidly, for example the Gilets Jaunes protests in France in 2018. In current debates about climate action, discourses emerge that justify inaction and the delay of action, for example by emphasizing the downsides of climate policies (Lamb et al. 2020). Such developments could be reflected in our results by the fact that the DISPUTE topic remained stable, or even increased in prevalence.

A close correspondence was found between media type, that is, type of internet channel, and topic. The topic DISPUTE and to a lesser extent the topic DESPAIR dominate Twitter, comments, and similar discussion sites. The topic SCIENCE as well as the topic GLOBAL WARMING are a dominant theme in journalistic articles, in popular science sites, and also in blogs. We suggest a dichotomy of topics between science oriented discourses and dispute-conflict oriented discourses. This dichotomy also largely holds with respect to media types. On the one hand, online articles and blogs are primarily outlets for scientific, argument-based debates, whereas tweets and comments are the primary channel for communicating dissent, conflict, polemics, and affective expressions. This correspondence is quite stable across the three points in time being studied.

As mentioned in the introduction, this finding is not unexpected. It has been often observed that social media are in large parts characterized by aggressive, impolite, and provocative forms of communication (Lünenborg 2019;Pearce et al. 2019;Walsh 2020;Zhuravskaya, Petrova, and Enikolopov 2020), and explanations range from communicative features such as social distance and anonymity, to technical-economic features which reward short, affective, and sensationalist messages. In our study, these features apply to media types Twitter/X and comments, and dispute and despair show up primarily on these media. Consequently, skepticism and climate change denial will be prominent in social media; however, the DISPUTE topic indicates that social media are not simply outlets of climate change skepticism, but at the same time function as platforms for climate change activism; hence, even though the discourse might be described as rough and lacking good manners, we find various ideological camps involved in the discourse and the role of Twitter and other social media might be more complex (Diehl et al. 2021). However, important social media such as Facebook, TikTok, or Instagram have not been included in our study, so we must be careful with generalizations.

As pointed out in the introduction, a promising theoretical account for the dichotomy between fact-based and affect-based discourses is Kahneman's (2011;Morewedge and Kahneman 2010) two-systems theory. Kahneman suggests two modalities or systems of cognition which process information differently and generate different types of judgments and evaluations. System 1 operates largely in a fast and automatic fashion, based on associations, is effortless, and is closely linked to affect and perception. System 2 operates mainly under conscious control, proceeds according to rules, logic, and rational arguments, but is slow and requires cognitive effort. System 1 generates intuitive judgments based on gut feeling, and System 2 generates judgments based on deliberation and analysis. The notion of cognitive effort may provide a key dimension to understand differences in topic prevalence and media type usage. Internet media such as Twitter/X and most likely other social media channels such as WhatsApp or Facebook require little or no effort to use. Typically, short messages are expected, and the social norm is that these messages require no justification; the predominant response is purely preferential (like or dislike) and effortless, which may explain the popularity of these types of social media. In contrast, internet channels such as blogs (contributions of the principal author, not the comments) and longer journalistic contributions require some literacy and often a lot of effort, deliberation, and rational argumentation. The adequate response is epistemic (true or false), requiring a minimum of cognitive effort to prove a claim incorrect or an argument incoherent. Hence, we speculate that the psychological distinction between System 1 and System 2 is to some extent reflected in the topical structure and in the media type correspondences of climate change discussions on the internet.

Some shortcomings of this study should be noted. The sampling procedure can be criticized for being somewhat ad hoc and not yielding a representative sample of texts. As already mentioned in Section 2.1, this is indeed problematic, though we think that the notion of representative sampling is basically unattainable in this context. A related shortcoming is the relatively small number of texts in our corpora; topic analyses often process thousands or even millions of documents scraped from the internet. However, we think that the claim that the sheer amount of data is sufficient to generate insightful findings is unwarranted (Grimmer, Roberts, and Stewart 2021).

The method of topic modeling has some genuine drawbacks, as mentioned above (see Section 2.3). First, it is purely inductive or bottom-up, that is, the results do not depend on any preconceived theoretical assumptions about the topic structure; an implication is that standard algorithms are unable to distinguish relevant patterns from noise (for attempts towards a more top-down procedure see, for example, Watanabe and Baturo 2024). Second, technically, there are numerous degrees of freedom to control the analysis, most of them lack strong theoretical justification until now. The number of topics to be extracted must be fixed a priori, without clear rules what the 'correct' number might be; in fact, we think that a correct number does not exist. A specific estimation algorithm must be selected from a plethora of currently available algorithms with each algorithm providing many optional parameters that control the estimation process (Wiedemann 2022); also, since the estimation process usually involves some random element, the results are not deterministic. Furthermore, how to evaluate the quality of a topic model is still debated, though measures such as coherence and exclusivity are conventionally used, and steps toward a more standardized approach have been proposed (Chen, Peng, et al. 2023;Maier et al. 2018). Ultimately, topic models provide a helpful approach to manage huge amounts of textual data, but the conceptual interpretation remains subjective and a matter of expertise with the substantive field.

Overall, our findings contribute to an understanding of online discourses about climate change and how they have evolved over time. The analysis highlights the importance of considering different media types and their influence on public opinion and behavior related to climate change. However, it remains challenging to derive some clear advice on policy-making and strategic agenda setting. Online media consumption is mostly driven by factors that attract attention and trigger affective responses (Robertson et al. 2023). This has always been the case also with traditional media, but online social media may intensify this property (Kasperson et al. 2022). More importantly, as digital media more and more replace traditional media, especially with younger generations, it is essential that political strategists and journalists adapt to this development and learn how to better reach their audiences.

A major concern in climate change debates is the widespread use of misinformation and the undermining of the credibility of scientists. This is particularly common on social media due to the lack of 'gatekeepers' and the unrestricted distribution of user generated content. Lewandowsky (2021) discusses several strategies how to communicate in what he calls 'adversarial environments' , for example, using emotional messages, highlighting scientific consensus, and using inoculation and debunking strategies. These measures are all the more important in online media, and should be adapted to social media in particular.

Another concern is that so called echo chambers and filter bubbles are easily formed on the internet, such as homogeneous groups on Facebook or Instagram. In echo chambers, like-minded people communicate only or mostly with each other, thus confirming and reinforcing their opinions and shielding themselves from opposing views and arguments (Williams et al. 2015). It has been assumed that echo chambers account for ideological polarization and radicalization tendencies. However, recent research by Nyhan and colleagues (Nyhan et al. 2023) suggests that this assumption may not be justified. Manipulating the amount of like-minded information available to Facebook users had no effect on political attitudes, ideological extremity or affective polarization. Also, the amount of political content shared on Facebook is relatively low. Whether online debates are the causes of political polarization, especially when shaped by social media constraints and affordances, or mainly reflect (and possibly amplify) preexisting attitudes and convictions, is an open question. Our findings underscore the need for further research in this area, as the topic of climate change will continue to be a subject of public controversy and debate.
